sections.heading: Run Autocomplete and Partial Match Queries
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run autocomplete and partial queries
that return results for partial words. This is useful to retrieve
results with increasing accuracy as more characters
are entered in your application's search field. You must
index the fields as the MongoDB Search [tokenization](bson-data-types-autocomplete) type to use the [autocomplete](autocomplete-ref)
operator.

To get started, see [How to Run Autocomplete and Partial Match MongoDB Search Queries](partial-match-tutorial).

sections.heading: Group Search Results with Facets
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run facet queries that group search results
by string, date, or numeric values. You must create an index
with a [facet definition](fts-facet-definition) to use the
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) collector.

To get started, see [How to Use Facets with MongoDB Search](facet-tutorial).

sections.heading: Use the MongoDB Search Playground
sections.body:
Use the [MongoDB Search Playground](https://search-playground.mongodb.com/).
to try different MongoDB Search features by configuring search indexes and running
queries without an Atlas account, cluster, or collection.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Search Playground](fts-playground).

sections.heading: Learn with a Course
sections.body:
To learn more about MongoDB Search, you can take Unit 1 of the
[Atlas Search Course on MongoDB University](https://learn.mongodb.com/learning-paths/atlas-search). The 2.25 hour unit includes an
overview of MongoDB Search and lessons on how to create MongoDB Search indexes, use
[$search](pipe.$search) with different operators, and generate search
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref).

sections.heading: Learn by Watching
sections.body:
You can also watch the following videos to
learn more about MongoDB Search:

Watch an overview of Atlas and MongoDB Search and get started setting up
MongoDB Search for your data. The video demonstrates how to load sample data on
your cluster, create a MongoDB Search index, and run a sample query using
Search Tester and Data Explorer.

*Duration: 10 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ](https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ)

Follow along with the following video to learn how to configure your
MongoDB Search index and run queries from your application.

*Duration: 7 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/3fRCD\_QptVQ](https://youtu.be/3fRCD_QptVQ)

Follow along with the following video tutorial walk-through that
demonstrates how to build MongoDB Search queries for a Restaurant Finder demo
application, which is also available at [www.atlassearchrestaurants.com](https://www.atlassearchrestaurants.com/).

*Duration: 20 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA](https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA)

MongoDB Search is an embedded full-text search feature
that gives you a seamless, scalable experience for building
relevance-based app features. This quick start describes
how to get started in the following steps:

- Create a MongoDB Search index on a sample collection.

- Build a MongoDB Search query to search the collection.
  You will learn how to:

  - Run a basic query to search for a term in a field

  - Use an operator to refine your search

  - Add a search option to process your results

*Time required: 15 minutes*

> **Note:** This tutorial includes examples for a selection of clients.
> For all supported clients, see the
> [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes) and
> [Queries](ref-query-management) pages.

a MongoDB Search index is a data structure that categorizes data in an easily
searchable format. It maps terms with documents that
contain those terms to enable fast retrieval of documents
at query-time.

You must configure a MongoDB Search index to query data using MongoDB Search.
We recommend that you index the fields that you regularly
use to sort or filter your data.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes).

In this section, you create a MongoDB Search index on the sample
[movies](mflix-movies) collection. You can create the
MongoDB Search index in either an Atlas cluster or a
deployment hosted on your local computer.

Atlas Cluster

- Connect to the cluster using [mongosh](mongodb-shell).

  Open [mongosh](mongodb-shell) in a terminal window and connect to your Atlas
  cluster. For detailed instructions on connecting, see
  [MongoDB Shell](connect-mongo-shell).

- Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.

  > **Example:** **Input:**
  >
  > ```shell
  > use sample_mflix
  > ```
  >
  > **Output:**
  >
  > ```shell
  > switched to db sample_mflix
  > ```

- Run the [db.collection.createSearchIndex()](manual/reference/method/db.collection.createSearchIndex) method to create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  db.movies.createSearchIndex(
     "default",
     { mappings: { dynamic: true } }
  )
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

Local Deployment

- Connect to the cluster using [mongosh](mongodb-shell).

  Open [mongosh](mongodb-shell) in a terminal window and connect to your Atlas
  cluster. For detailed instructions on connecting, see
  [MongoDB Shell](connect-mongo-shell).

- Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.

  > **Example:** **Input:**
  >
  > ```shell
  > use sample_mflix
  > ```
  >
  > **Output:**
  >
  > ```shell
  > switched to db sample_mflix
  > ```

- Run the [db.collection.createSearchIndex()](manual/reference/method/db.collection.createSearchIndex) method to create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  db.movies.createSearchIndex(
     "default",
     { mappings: { dynamic: true } }
  )
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

Run a basic `$search` query by using the
[text](text-ref) operator.

This query has the following search criteria:

- The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
  [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
  to return only 3 documents and the `title` and `plot` fields.

**Input:**

```javascript
db.movies.aggregate([
  {
    $search:
    {
      "text": {
        "query": "baseball",
        "path": "plot"
      }
    }
  },
  {
    $limit: 3
  },
  {
    $project: {
      "_id": 0,
      "title": 1,
      "plot": 1
    }
  }
])
```

**Output:**

```shell
{
  "plot" : "A trio of guys try and make up for missed
  opportunities in childhood by forming a three-player
  baseball team to compete against standard children
  baseball squads.",
  "title" : "The Benchwarmers"
}
{
  "plot" : "A young boy is bequeathed the ownership of a
  professional baseball team.",
  "title" : "Little Big League"
}
{
  "plot" : "A trained chimpanzee plays third base for a
  minor-league baseball team.",
  "title" : "Ed"
}
```

MongoDB Search provides several [operators](fts-operators) that you
can use to build complex queries and retrieve more specific
results for your use case. Update your `$search` query to use the
[compound](compound-ref) operator to combine
multiple operators into a single query.

This query has the following search criteria:

- The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

- The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
  `Romance`.

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
  [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
  to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`, and
  `genres` fields.

**Input:**

```javascript
db.movies.aggregate([
  {
    $search: {
      "compound": {
        "must": [
          {
            "text": {
              "query": "baseball",
              "path": "plot"
            }
          }
        ],
        "mustNot": [
          {
            "text": {
              "query": ["Comedy", "Romance"],
              "path": "genres"
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  {
    $limit: 3
  },
  {
    $project: {
      "_id": 0,
      "title": 1,
      "plot": 1,
      "genres": 1
    }
  }
])
```

**Output:**

```shell
[
  {
    plot: 'The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'Family' ],
    title: 'The Pride of the Yankees'
  },
  {
    plot: 'Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport' ],
    title: 'The Babe'
  },
  {
    plot: 'Dominican baseball star Miguel "Sugar" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues.',
    genres: [ 'Drama', 'Sport' ],
    title: 'Sugar'
  }
]
```

MongoDB Search provides several [search options](fts-search-options)
that you can use to further process your MongoDB Search query results.
Add the [sorting](sort-ref) option to your query to
display the results in a specific order.

This query has the following search criteria:

- The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

- The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
  `Romance`.

- Sort the results by the `released` date
  field in descending order.
  MongoDB Search returns the most recent movies first.

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
  [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
  to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`,
  `genres`, and `released` fields.

**Input:**

```shell
db.movies.aggregate([
  {
    $search: {
      "compound": {
        "must": [ {
          "text": {
            "query": "baseball",
            "path": "plot"
          }
        }],
        "mustNot": [ {
          "text": {
            "query": ["Comedy", "Romance"],
            "path": "genres"
          }
        } ]
      },
      "sort": {
        "released": -1
      }
    }
  },
  {
    $limit: 3
  },
  {
    $project: {
      "_id": 0,
      "title": 1,
      "plot": 1,
      "genres": 1,
      "released": 1
    }
  }
])
```

**Output:**

```shell
[
  {
    plot: 'A sports agent stages an unconventional recruitment strategy to get talented Indian cricket players to play Major League Baseball.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport' ],
    title: 'Million Dollar Arm',
    released: ISODate('2014-05-16T00:00:00.000Z')
  },
  {
    plot: 'A Taiwanese high school baseball team travels to Japan in 1931 to compete in a national tournament.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'History' ],
    title: 'Kano',
    released: ISODate('2014-02-27T00:00:00.000Z')
  },
  {
    plot: "12-year-old Josh is a mixed race boy and a promising baseball player...",
    genres: [ 'Drama' ],
    title: 'Calloused Hands',
    released: ISODate('2013-03-03T00:00:00.000Z')
  }
]
```

learn more about MongoDB Search:

[https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ](https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ)

Follow along with the following video to learn how to configure your
MongoDB Search index and run queries from your application.

*Duration: 7 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/3fRCD\_QptVQ](https://youtu.be/3fRCD_QptVQ)

Follow along with the following video tutorial walk-through that
demonstrates how to build MongoDB Search queries for a Restaurant Finder demo
application, which is also available at [www.atlassearchrestaurants.com](https://www.atlassearchrestaurants.com/).

*Duration: 20 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA](https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA)

MongoDB Search is an embedded full-text search feature
that gives you a seamless, scalable experience for building
relevance-based app features. This quick start describes
how to get started in the following steps:

- Create a MongoDB Search index on a sample collection.

- Build a MongoDB Search query to search the collection.
  You will learn how to:

  - Run a basic query to search for a term in a field

  - Use an operator to refine your search

  - Add a search option to process your results

*Time required: 15 minutes*

> **Note:** This tutorial includes examples for a selection of clients.
> For all supported clients, see the
> [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes) and
> [Queries](ref-query-management) pages.

a MongoDB Search index is a data structure that categorizes data in an easily
searchable format. It maps terms with documents that
contain those terms to enable fast retrieval of documents
at query-time.

You must configure a MongoDB Search index to query data using MongoDB Search.
We recommend that you index the fields that you regularly
use to sort or filter your data.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes).

In this section, you create a MongoDB Search index on the sample
[movies](mflix-movies) collection. You can create the
MongoDB Search index in either an Atlas cluster or a
deployment hosted on your local computer.

Atlas Cluster

Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

On the Database screen, click the sample\_mflix database, then click the movies collection.

- Click the Indexes tab, then select Search Indexes.

- Click Create Index to open the index creation dialog box.

- Use the default index name and definition.

  ```json
  {
   mappings: {
     dynamic: true,
   },
  }
  ```

- Click Create Search Index.

Local Deployment

Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

On the Database screen, click the sample\_mflix database, then click the movies collection.

- Click the Indexes tab, then select Search Indexes.

- Click Create Index to open the index creation dialog box.

- Specify a name for the index.

  Your MongoDB Search index is named `default` by default. If you keep this name, then your index will be the
  default Search index for any MongoDB Search query that does not specify a different `index` option in
  its [operators](fts-operators). If you are creating multiple indexes, we recommend
  that you maintain a consistent, descriptive naming convention across your indexes.

- Specify the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) MongoDB Search index definition.

  ```json
  {
    "mappings": { 
      "dynamic": true|false, 
      "fields": { 
        "<field-name>": {
          "type": "string",
          "analyzer": "<atlas-search-analyzer>",
          "searchAnalyzer": "<atlas-search-analyzer>",
          "indexOptions": "docs|freqs|positions|offsets",
          "store": true|false,
          "ignoreAbove": <integer>,
          "similarity": { "type": "bm25|boolean|stableTfl" },
          "multi": { <string-field-definition> },
          "norms": "include|omit"
        }
      } 
    }
  }
  ```

- Click Create Search Index.

MongoDB Search queries take the form of an [aggregation pipeline stage](manual/aggregation). You use MongoDB Search primarily with the [$search](pipe.$search) stage,
which must be the first stage in the query pipeline. You can also use
this stage in conjunction with other stages in your pipeline.

When you run a MongoDB Search query, MongoDB Search uses the search index to
locate and retrieve relevant data from the collection.
MongoDB Search also provides the [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage,
multiple sub-pipelines, and several query
[MongoDB Search operators](operators-ref) and [collectors](collectors-ref)
that you can use to further refine your search results.

To learn more, see [Queries and Indexes](fts-about-queries).

In this section, you run queries against the indexed collection.

On the Database screen, click the `sample_mflix`
database, then click the `movies` collection.

Run a basic `$search` query by using the
[text](text-ref) operator.

To run this query in MongoDB Compass:

- Click the Aggregations tab.

- Click Select..., then configure each of the following
  pipeline stages by selecting the stage from the dropdown and adding
  the query for that stage. Click Add Stage to add
  additional stages.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title` and `plot` fields.

  | Pipeline Stage | Query                                                 |
  | -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
  | $search        | \{ "text": \{ "query": "baseball", "path": "plot" } } |
  | $limit         | 3                                                     |
  | $project       | \{ "\_id": 0, "title": 1, "plot": 1, }                |

If you enabled Auto Preview, MongoDB Compass displays the
following documents next to the `$project`
pipeline stage:

```shell
 {
   "plot" : "A trio of guys try and make up for missed opportunities in childhood by forming a three-player baseball team to compete against standard children baseball squads.",
   "title" : "The Benchwarmers"
 }
 {
   "plot" : "A young boy is bequeathed the ownership of a professional baseball team.",
   "title" : "Little Big League"
 }
 {
   "plot" : "A trained chimpanzee plays third base for a minor-league baseball team.",
   "title" : "Ed"
 }
```

MongoDB Search provides several [operators](fts-operators) that you
can use to build complex queries and retrieve more specific
results for your use case. Update your `$search` query to use the
[compound](compound-ref) operator to combine
multiple operators into a single query.

This query has the following search criteria:

- The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

- The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
  `Romance`.

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
  [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
  to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`, and
  `genres` fields.

Update the following pipeline stages in MongoDB Compass:

| Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                         |
| -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| $search        | \{ "compound": \{ "must": \[ \{ "text": \{ "query": "baseball", "path": "plot" } } ], "mustNot": \[ \{ "text": \{ "query": \["Comedy", "Romance"], "path": "genres" } } ] } } |
| $project       | \{ "\_id": 0, "title": 1, "plot": 1, "genres": 1, }                                                                                                                           |

If you enabled Auto Preview, MongoDB Compass displays the
following documents next to the `$project`
pipeline stage:

```shell
 {
   "plot" : "The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig.",
   "genres" : [ "Biography", "Drama", "Family" ],
   "title" : "The Pride of the Yankees"
 }
 {
   "plot" : "Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him.",
   "genres" : [ "Biography", "Drama", "Sport" ],
   "title" : "The Babe"
 }
 {
   "plot" : "Dominican baseball star Miguel \"Sugar\" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues.",
   "genres" : [ "Drama", "Sport" ],
   "title" : "Sugar"
 }
```

MongoDB Search provides several [search options](fts-search-options)
that you can use to further process your MongoDB Search query results.
Add the [sorting](sort-ref) option to your query to
display the results in a specific order.

This query has the following search criteria:

- The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

- The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
  `Romance`.

- Sort the results by the `released` date
  field in descending order.
  MongoDB Search returns the most recent movies first.

- [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
  [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
  to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`,
  `genres`, and `released` fields.

Update the following pipeline stages in MongoDB Compass:

| Pipeline Stage | Query                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| $search        | \{ "compound": \{ "must": \[ \{ "text": \{ "query": "baseball", "path": "plot" } } ], "mustNot": \[ \{ "text": \{ "query": \["Comedy", "Romance"], "path": "genres" } } ] }, "sort": \{ "released": -1 } } |
| $project       | \{ "\_id": 0, "title": 1, "plot": 1, "genres": 1, "released": 1, }                                                                                                                                         |

If you enabled Auto Preview, MongoDB Compass displays the
following documents next to the `$project` pipeline stage:

```shell
[
  {
    plot: 'A sports agent stages an unconventional recruitment strategy to get talented Indian cricket players to play Major League Baseball.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport' ],
    title: 'Million Dollar Arm',
    released: 2014-05-16T00:00:00.000+00:00
  },
  {
    plot: 'A Taiwanese high school baseball team travels to Japan in 1931 to compete in a national tournament.',
    genres: [ 'Biography', 'Drama', 'History' ],
    title: 'Kano',
    released: 2014-02-27T00:00:00.000+00:00
  },
  {
    plot: "12-year-old Josh is a mixed race boy and a promising baseball player...",
    genres: [ 'Drama' ],
    title: 'Calloused Hands',
    released: 2013-03-03T00:00:00.000+00:00
  }
]
```

sections.heading: Run Autocomplete and Partial Match Queries
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run autocomplete and partial queries
that return results for partial words. This is useful to retrieve
results with increasing accuracy as more characters
are entered in your application's search field. You must
index the fields as the MongoDB Search [tokenization](bson-data-types-autocomplete) type to use the [autocomplete](autocomplete-ref)
operator.

To get started, see [How to Run Autocomplete and Partial Match MongoDB Search Queries](partial-match-tutorial).

sections.heading: Group Search Results with Facets
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run facet queries that group search results
by string, date, or numeric values. You must create an index
with a [facet definition](fts-facet-definition) to use the
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) collector.

To get started, see [How to Use Facets with MongoDB Search](facet-tutorial).

sections.heading: Learn by Watching
sections.body:
You can also watch the following videos to
learn more about MongoDB Search:

Watch an overview of Atlas and MongoDB Search and get started setting up
MongoDB Search for your data. The video demonstrates how to load sample data on
your cluster, create a MongoDB Search index, and run a sample query using
Search Tester and Data Explorer.

*Duration: 10 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ](https://youtu.be/HsS0z3eOCSQ)

Follow along with the following video to learn how to configure your
MongoDB Search index and run queries from your application.

*Duration: 7 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/3fRCD\_QptVQ](https://youtu.be/3fRCD_QptVQ)

Follow along with the following video tutorial walk-through that
demonstrates how to build MongoDB Search queries for a Restaurant Finder demo
application, which is also available at [www.atlassearchrestaurants.com](https://www.atlassearchrestaurants.com/).

*Duration: 20 Minutes*

[https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA](https://youtu.be/s2kXYZRE7pA)

MongoDB Search is an embedded full-text search feature
that gives you a seamless, scalable experience for building
relevance-based app features. This quick start describes
how to get started in the following steps:

- Create a MongoDB Search index on a sample collection.

- Build a MongoDB Search query to search the collection.
  You will learn how to:

  - Run a basic query to search for a term in a field

  - Use an operator to refine your search

  - Add a search option to process your results

*Time required: 15 minutes*

> **Note:** This tutorial includes examples for a selection of clients.
> For all supported clients, see the
> [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes) and
> [Queries](ref-query-management) pages.

a MongoDB Search index is a data structure that categorizes data in an easily
searchable format. It maps terms with documents that
contain those terms to enable fast retrieval of documents
at query-time.

You must configure a MongoDB Search index to query data using MongoDB Search.
We recommend that you index the fields that you regularly
use to sort or filter your data.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Search indexes](fts-manage-indexes).

In this section, you create a MongoDB Search index on the sample
[movies](mflix-movies) collection. You can create the
MongoDB Search index in either an Atlas cluster or a
deployment hosted on your local computer.

Atlas Cluster

- Install the MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver.

  Create a new Kotlin project and install the
  [MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver documentation](kotlin/coroutine/current/getting-started).

- Define the index.

  Create a file named `CreateIndex.kt`. Copy and paste the following
  code into the file.

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.MongoException
  import com.mongodb.client.model.SearchIndexModel
  import com.mongodb.client.model.SearchIndexType
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document

  fun main() {
      // Replace the placeholder with your MongoDB deployment's connection string
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")
      val searchIdx = Document(
          "mappings",
          Document("dynamic", true)
      )
      runBlocking {
          try {
              val result = collection.createSearchIndex("default", searchIdx)
              println("Index created: $result")
          } catch (e: MongoException) {
              println("Failed to create search index: ${e.message}")
          } finally {
              mongoClient.close()
          }
      }
  }
  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Run the `CreateIndex.kt` file in your IDE.

Local Deployment

- Install the MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver.

  Create a new Kotlin project and install the
  [MongoDB Kotlin Coroutine Driver documentation](kotlin/coroutine/current/getting-started).

- Define the index.

  Create a file named `CreateIndex.kt`. Copy and paste the following
  code into the file.

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.MongoException
  import com.mongodb.client.model.SearchIndexModel
  import com.mongodb.client.model.SearchIndexType
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document

  fun main() {
      // Replace the placeholder with your MongoDB deployment's connection string
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")
      val searchIdx = Document(
          "mappings",
          Document("dynamic", true)
      )
      runBlocking {
          try {
              val result = collection.createSearchIndex("default", searchIdx)
              println("Index created: $result")
          } catch (e: MongoException) {
              println("Failed to create search index: ${e.message}")
          } finally {
              mongoClient.close()
          }
      }
  }
  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Run the `CreateIndex.kt` file in your IDE.

MongoDB Search queries take the form of an [aggregation pipeline stage](manual/aggregation). You use MongoDB Search primarily with the [$search](pipe.$search) stage,
which must be the first stage in the query pipeline. You can also use
this stage in conjunction with other stages in your pipeline.

When you run a MongoDB Search query, MongoDB Search uses the search index to
locate and retrieve relevant data from the collection.
MongoDB Search also provides the [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage,
multiple sub-pipelines, and several query
[MongoDB Search operators](operators-ref) and [collectors](collectors-ref)
that you can use to further refine your search results.

To learn more, see [Queries and Indexes](fts-about-queries).

In this section, you run queries against the indexed collection.

Run a basic `$search` query by using the
[text](text-ref) operator.

- Create a new file named `RunQuery.kt` and paste the following code.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title` and `plot` fields.

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.limit
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.project
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Filters.eq
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Projections.*
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document

  fun main() {
      // establish connection and set namespace
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // define query
          val agg = Document("query", "baseball").append("path","plot")

          // run query and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(
                  eq("\$search", eq("text", agg)),
                  limit(3),
                  project(fields(excludeId(), include("title", "plot")))
              )
          )
          resultsFlow.collect { println(it) }
      }
      mongoClient.close()
  }
  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  When you run the `RunQuery.kt` program in your IDE, it
  prints the following documents:

  ```none
  Document{{plot=The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig., genres=[Biography, Drama, Family], title=The Pride of the Yankees}}
  Document{{plot=Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him., genres=[Biography, Drama, Sport], title=The Babe}}
  Document{{plot=Dominican baseball star Miguel "Sugar" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues., genres=[Drama, Sport], title=Sugar}}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [operators](fts-operators) that you
can use to build complex queries and retrieve more specific
results for your use case. Update your `$search` query to use the
[compound](compound-ref) operator to combine
multiple operators into a single query.

- Modify `RunQuery.kt` to use the compound query.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`, and
    `genres` fields.

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.limit
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.project
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Filters.eq
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Projections.*
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document

  fun main() {
      // establish connection and set namespace
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // define query
          val compoundQuery = Document(
              "must", listOf(
                  Document("text", Document("query", "baseball")
                      .append("path", "plot"))
              )
          )
              .append(
                  "mustNot", listOf(
                      Document("text", Document("query", listOf("Comedy", "Romance"))
                          .append("path", "genres"))
                  )
              )

          // run query and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(
                  eq("\$search", eq("compound", compoundQuery)),
                  limit(3),
                  project(fields(
                      excludeId(),
                      include("title", "plot", "genres")
                  ))
              )
          )
          resultsFlow.collect { println(it) }
      }
      mongoClient.close()
  }

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query.

  When you run the `RunQuery.kt` program in your IDE, it
  prints the following documents:

  ```none
  Document{{plot=The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig., genres=[Biography, Drama, Family], title=The Pride of the Yankees}}
  Document{{plot=Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him., genres=[Biography, Drama, Sport], title=The Babe}}
  Document{{plot=Dominican baseball star Miguel "Sugar" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues., genres=[Drama, Sport], title=Sugar}}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [search options](fts-search-options)
that you can use to further process your MongoDB Search query results.
Add the [sorting](sort-ref) option to your query to
display the results in a specific order.

- Modify `RunQuery.kt` to add the [sorting](sort-ref) option.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - Sort the results by the `released` date
    field in descending order.
    MongoDB Search returns the most recent movies first.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`,
    `genres`, and `released` fields.

  ```kotlin
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.limit
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Aggregates.project
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Filters.eq
  import com.mongodb.client.model.Projections.*
  import com.mongodb.kotlin.client.coroutine.MongoClient
  import kotlinx.coroutines.runBlocking
  import org.bson.Document

  fun main() {
      // establish connection and set namespace
      val uri = "<connection-string>"
      val mongoClient = MongoClient.create(uri)
      val database = mongoClient.getDatabase("sample_mflix")
      val collection = database.getCollection<Document>("movies")

      runBlocking {
          // define query
          val compoundQuery = Document(
              "must", listOf(
                  Document("text", Document("query", "baseball")
                      .append("path", "plot"))
              )
          )
              .append(
                  "mustNot", listOf(
                      Document("text", Document("query", listOf("Comedy", "Romance"))
                          .append("path", "genres"))
                  )
              )
          
          // sort configuration
          val sortConfig = Document("released", -1)

          // run query and print results
          val resultsFlow = collection.aggregate<Document>(
              listOf(
                  Document("\$search", Document("compound", compoundQuery).append("sort", sortConfig)),
                  limit(3),
                  project(fields(
                      excludeId(),
                      include("title", "plot", "genres", "released")
                  ))
              )
          )
          resultsFlow.collect { println(it) }
      }
      mongoClient.close()
  }

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  When you run the `RunQuery.kt` program in your IDE, it
  prints the following documents:

  ```none
  Document{{plot=A sports agent stages an unconventional recruitment strategy to get talented Indian cricket players to play Major League Baseball., genres=[Biography, Drama, Sport], title=Million Dollar Arm, released=Thu May 15 19:00:00 CDT 2014}}
  Document{{plot=A Taiwanese high school baseball team travels to Japan in 1931 to compete in a national tournament., genres=[Biography, Drama, History], title=Kano, released=Wed Feb 26 18:00:00 CST 2014}}
  Document{{plot=12-year-old Josh is a mixed race boy and a promising baseball player. He is abused by his mother's boyfriend Byrd, and neglected by his mother Debbie. He forges his own path in life when ..., genres=[Drama], title=Calloused Hands, released=Sat Mar 02 18:00:00 CST 2013}}
  ```

- Build a MongoDB Search query to search the collection.
  You will learn how to:

  - Run a basic query to search for a term in a field

*Time required: 15 minutes*

a MongoDB Search index is a data structure that categorizes data in an easily
searchable format. It maps terms with documents that
contain those terms to enable fast retrieval of documents
at query-time.

You must configure a MongoDB Search index to query data using MongoDB Search.

In this section, you create a MongoDB Search index on the sample
[movies](mflix-movies) collection. You can create the
MongoDB Search index in either an Atlas cluster or a
deployment hosted on your local computer.

Atlas Cluster

- Install the MongoDB Python Driver.

  ```sh
  pip install pymongo
  ```

  For detailed installation instructions, see
  [MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

- Define the index.

  Paste the following code into a file named `create_index.py`.

  ```python
  from pymongo import MongoClient
  from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

  # connect to your Atlas deployment
  uri = "<connection-string>"
  client = MongoClient(uri)

  # set namespace
  database = client["sample_mflix"]
  collection = database["movies"]

  # define your MongoDB Search index
  search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
      definition={
          "mappings": {
              "dynamic": True
          },
      },
      name="default",
  )

  # create the index
  result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
  print(result)

  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  python create_index.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

Local Deployment

- Install the MongoDB Python Driver.

  ```sh
  pip install pymongo
  ```

  For detailed installation instructions, see
  [MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

- Define the index.

  Paste the following code into a file named `create_index.py`.

  ```python
  from pymongo import MongoClient
  from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

  # connect to your Atlas deployment
  uri = "<connection-string>"
  client = MongoClient(uri)

  # set namespace
  database = client["sample_mflix"]
  collection = database["movies"]

  # define your MongoDB Search index
  search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
      definition={
          "mappings": {
              "dynamic": True
          },
      },
      name="default",
  )

  # create the index
  result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
  print(result)

  ```

  Atlas Cluster

  Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
  Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

  Atlas Cluster

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
  ```

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local or Self-Managed

  Your connection string should use the
  following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

  To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

  IMPORTANT: Ensure that your connection string includes your database
  user's credentials. To learn more about finding your connection string,
  see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

  Local Deployment

  Your connection string should use the following format:

  ```
  mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
  ```

- Create the index.

  **Input:**

  ```shell
  python create_index.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```
  default
  ```

MongoDB Search queries take the form of an [aggregation pipeline stage](manual/aggregation). You use MongoDB Search primarily with the [$search](pipe.$search) stage,
which must be the first stage in the query pipeline. You can also use
this stage in conjunction with other stages in your pipeline.

When you run a MongoDB Search query, MongoDB Search uses the search index to
locate and retrieve relevant data from the collection.
MongoDB Search also provides the [$searchMeta](pipe.$searchMeta) stage,
multiple sub-pipelines, and several query
[MongoDB Search operators](operators-ref) and [collectors](collectors-ref)
that you can use to further refine your search results.

To learn more, see [Queries and Indexes](fts-about-queries).

In this section, you run queries against the indexed collection.

Run a basic `$search` query by using the
[text](text-ref) operator.

- Create a new file named `run_query.py` and paste the following code.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title` and `plot` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': 
       {
          'text': {
            'query': 'baseball',
            'path': 'plot'
          }
       }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    }, 
    {
      '$project': {
         '_id': 0,
         'title': 1,
         'plot': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'A trio of guys try and make up for missed opportunities in childhood by forming a three-player baseball team to compete against standard children baseball squads.', 'title': 'The Benchwarmers'}
  {'plot': 'A young boy is bequeathed the ownership of a professional baseball team.', 'title': 'Little Big League'}
  {'plot': 'A trained chimpanzee plays third base for a minor-league baseball team.', 'title': 'Ed'}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [operators](fts-operators) that you
can use to build complex queries and retrieve more specific
results for your use case. Update your `$search` query to use the
[compound](compound-ref) operator to combine
multiple operators into a single query.

- Modify `run_query.py` to use the compound query.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`, and
    `genres` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': {
        'compound': {
          'must': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': 'baseball',
                'path': 'plot'
              }
            }
          ],
          'mustNot': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': ['Comedy', 'Romance'],
                'path': 'genres'
              }
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    },
    {
      '$project': {
        '_id': 0,           
        'title': 1,
        'plot': 1,
        'genres': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'The story of the life and career of the famed baseball player, Lou Gehrig.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Family'], 'title': 'The Pride of the Yankees'}
  {'plot': 'Babe Ruth becomes a baseball legend but is unheroic to those who know him.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'The Babe'}
  {'plot': 'Dominican baseball star Miguel "Sugar" Santos is recruited to play in the U.S. minor-leagues.', 'genres': ['Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'Sugar'}
  ```

MongoDB Search provides several [search options](fts-search-options)
that you can use to further process your MongoDB Search query results.
Add the [sorting](sort-ref) option to your query to
display the results in a specific order.

- Modify `run_query.py` to add the [sorting](sort-ref) option.

  This query has the following search criteria:

  - The `plot` field must contain the word `baseball`.

  - The `genres` field must not contain either `Comedy` or
    `Romance`.

  - Sort the results by the `released` date
    field in descending order.
    MongoDB Search returns the most recent movies first.

  - [$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) and
    [$project](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/project) stages
    to return only 3 documents and the `title`, `plot`,
    `genres`, and `released` fields.

  ```python
  import pymongo

  client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')
  result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate([
    {
      '$search': {
        'compound': {
          'must': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': 'baseball',
                'path': 'plot'
              }
            }
          ],
          'mustNot': [
            {
              'text': {
                'query': ['Comedy', 'Romance'],
                'path': 'genres'
              }
            }
          ]
        },
        'sort': {
            'released': -1
        }
      }
    }, 
    {
      '$limit': 3
    },
    {
      '$project': {
        '_id': 0,           
        'title': 1,
        'plot': 1,
        'genres': 1,
        'released': 1
      }
    }
  ])

  for i in result:
      print(i)

  ```

- Specify the `<connection-string>`, then run the query:

  **Input:**

  ```bash
  python run_query.py
  ```

  **Output:**

  ```shell
  {'plot': 'A sports agent stages an unconventional recruitment strategy to get talented Indian cricket players to play Major League Baseball.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'Sport'], 'title': 'Million Dollar Arm', 'released': datetime.datetime(2014, 5, 16, 0, 0)}
  {'plot': 'A Taiwanese high school baseball team travels to Japan in 1931 to compete in a national tournament.', 'genres': ['Biography', 'Drama', 'History'], 'title': 'Kano', 'released': datetime.datetime(2014, 2, 27, 0, 0)}
  {'plot': "12-year-old Josh is a mixed race boy and a promising baseball player...", 'genres': ['Drama'], 'title': 'Calloused Hands', 'released': datetime.datetime(2013, 3, 3, 0, 0)}
  ```

sections.heading: Run Autocomplete and Partial Match Queries
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run autocomplete and partial queries
that return results for partial words. This is useful to retrieve
results with increasing accuracy as more characters
are entered in your application's search field. You must
index the fields as the MongoDB Search [tokenization](bson-data-types-autocomplete) type to use the [autocomplete](autocomplete-ref)
operator.

To get started, see [How to Run Autocomplete and Partial Match MongoDB Search Queries](partial-match-tutorial).

sections.heading: Group Search Results with Facets
sections.body:
You can use MongoDB Search to run facet queries that group search results
by string, date, or numeric values. You must create an index
with a [facet definition](fts-facet-definition) to use the
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) collector.

To get started, see [How to Use Facets with MongoDB Search](facet-tutorial).

This quick start demonstrates how to deploy MongoDB Search and MongoDB Vector

for the sample data, and run search queries against the sample data.

*Time required: 20 minutes*

This quick start walks you through the following steps:

- Deploy MongoDB and `mongot` in Docker.

  For more details on deploying by using a tarball or as an image in Docker,
  see [Search and Vector Search](manual/administration/install-community).

- Load sample data, create search index, and run search queries on your local deployment.

> **Note:** The resulting set up of this quick start is not secure. For more information
> on securing your deployment, see [Security for Self-Managed Deployments](manual/administration/security-checklist).

sections.heading: Architecture
sections.body:
This tutorial creates the following architecture:

- A MongoDB Community Edition Server (`mongod`) with a single node replica set
  on port 27017

- A MongoDB Search (`mongot`) search engine component on port 27028

- Persistant data volumes on both ports

- Pre-loaded sample data

> **Note:** For a production deployment, MongoDB recommends that you use a replica
> set with at least three members.

Before you begin, you must complete the following prerequisites:

- Download Docker v4.40 or higher

- Download Docker Compose

- Download the `curl` command

- Download `mongosh` locally or have access to it through Docker

sections.heading: Step 1: Pull the MongoDB Search Docker image and the latest MongoDB Community Edition image
sections.body:
To download the `mongot` Docker image and the latest MongoDB Community
Edition image, run the following command:

```shell
docker pull mongodb/mongodb-community-server:latest && docker pull mongodb/mongodb-community-search:latest
```

> **Note:** You must have a minimum of MongoDB 8.2.0 to use MongoDB Search with a
> self-managed deployment. For more information, see [MongoDB Search Compatibility](fts-feature-availability).

sections.heading: Step 2: Download an archive of sample data
sections.body:
To download the sample data, run the following command:

```shell
curl https://atlas-education.s3.amazonaws.com/sampledata.archive -o sampledata.archive
```

sections.heading: Step 3: Create your password file
sections.body:
Create a password file for `mongot` to connect to `mongod`.

> **Note:** Do not include trailing newlines in your password file.
> `mongot` rejects password files that contain trailing newlines.

Select your operating system and run the command to create your password file:

Linux/MacOS

```shell
echo -n "mongotPassword" > pwfile
chmod 400 pwfile
```

> **Note:** The `-n` flag prevents a trailing newline.

Windows Command Prompt

```shell
echo|set /p="mongotPassword" > pwfile
```

New files created in Windows inherit permissions from the
parent folder. Run the following commands to reset
permissions and set read-only permissions for the local
group `docker-users`:

```shell
SET pwpath=.\pwfile

# Reset permissions to remove explicit permissions
icacls.exe %pwpath% /reset
# Grant read-only permissions to docker users group
icacls.exe %pwpath% /GRANT:R "docker-users:(R)"
# Disable inheritance and remove inherited permissions
icacls.exe %pwpath% /inheritance:r
```

Windows PowerShell

```shell
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText("pwfile", "mongotPassword")
```

New files created in Windows inherit permissions from the
parent folder. Run the following commands to reset
permissions and set read-only permissions for the local
group `docker-users`:

```shell
SET pwpath=.\pwfile

# Reset permissions to remove explicit permissions
icacls.exe %pwpath% /reset
# Grant read-only permissions to docker users group
icacls.exe %pwpath% /GRANT:R "docker-users:(R)"
# Disable inheritance and remove inherited permissions
icacls.exe %pwpath% /inheritance:r
```

> **Note:** 
>
> - If you change the `mongot` password, you must also update the
>   password value in the `init-mongo-sh` script.
>
> - Most text editors automatically add newlines to files when you
>   edit files manually.

sections.heading: Step 4: Set up your configuration files
sections.body:
Create the following configuration files in your project folder:

```shell
services:
   mongod:
      image: mongodb/mongodb-community-server:latest
      container_name: mongod-community
      command: >-
         mongod
         --config /etc/mongod.conf
         --replSetMember=mongod.search-community:27017
      ports:
         - 27017:27017
      extra_hosts:
         - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"
      volumes:
         - mongod_data:/data/db
         - ./mongod.conf:/etc/mongod.conf:ro
         - ./sampledata.archive:/sampledata.archive
         - ./init-mongo.sh:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init-mongo.sh:ro
      networks:
         - search-community


   mongot:
      image: mongodb/mongodb-community-search:latest
      container_name: mongot-community-pupr
      networks:
         - search-community
      volumes:
         - mongot_data:/data/mongot
         - ./mongot.conf:/mongot-community/config.default.yml
         - ./pwfile:/mongot-community/pwfile:ro
      depends_on:
         - mongod
      ports:
         - 27028:27028  # Query server port from config
         - 9946:9946    # Metrics port from config

volumes:
   mongod_data:
   mongot_data:

networks:
   search-community:
      name: search-community
      external: true  # Use an external network if it exists. Comment this line if you want to create a new network.
```

> **Note:** If you specified a custom password in your password file, update the
> `mongotPassword` in the `init-mongo.sh` file to your custom password.
>
> The `mongotUser` in this file is only in use for the `mongod` to
> authorize `mongot` through the `searchCoordinator` role.

```shell
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "Starting MongoDB initialization..."
sleep 2

# Create user using local connection (no port specification needed)
echo "Creating user..."
mongosh --eval "
const adminDb = db.getSiblingDB('admin');
try {
adminDb.createUser({
   user: 'mongotUser',
   pwd: 'mongotPassword',
   roles: [{ role: 'searchCoordinator', db: 'admin' }]
});
print('User mongotUser created successfully');
} catch (error) {
if (error.code === 11000) {
   print('User mongotUser already exists');
} else {
   print('Error creating user: ' + error);
}
}
"

# Check for existing data
echo "Checking for existing sample data..."
if mongosh --quiet --eval "db.getSiblingDB('sample_airbnb').getCollectionNames().includes('listingsAndReviews')" | grep -q "true"; then
echo "Sample data already exists. Skipping restore."
else
echo "Sample data not found. Running mongorestore..."
if [ -f "/sampledata.archive" ]; then
   mongorestore --archive=/sampledata.archive
   echo "Sample data restored successfully."
else
   echo "Warning: sampledata.archive not found"
fi
fi

echo "MongoDB initialization completed."
```

> **Note:** If you deploy `mongot` in a production environment,
> MongoDB recommends that you enable security and configure TLS. For
> more information about `mongod` security settings, see
> [Self-Managed Security](manual/core/self-managed-security).

```shell
# mongod.conf
storage:
   dbPath: /data/db

net:
   port: 27017
   bindIp: 0.0.0.0

setParameter:
   searchIndexManagementHostAndPort: mongot.search-community:27028
   mongotHost: mongot.search-community:27028
   skipAuthenticationToSearchIndexManagementServer: false
   useGrpcForSearch: true

replication:
   replSetName: rs0
```

```shell
syncSource:
   replicaSet:
      hostAndPort: "mongod.search-community:27017"
      username: mongotUser
      passwordFile: /mongot-community/pwfile
      authSource: admin
      tls: false
      readPreference: primaryPreferred
storage:
   dataPath: "data/mongot"
server:
   grpc:
      address: "mongot.search-community:27028"
      tls:
         mode: "disabled"
metrics:
   enabled: true
   address: "mongot.search-community:9946"
healthCheck:
   address: "mongot.search-community:8080"
logging:
   verbosity: INFO
```

   search-community:
      name: search-community
      external: true  # Use an external network if it exists. Comment this line if you want to create a new network.

# mongod.conf
storage:
   dbPath: /data/db

net:
   port: 27017
   bindIp: 0.0.0.0

setParameter:
   searchIndexManagementHostAndPort: mongot.search-community:27028
   mongotHost: mongot.search-community:27028
   skipAuthenticationToSearchIndexManagementServer: false
   useGrpcForSearch: true

replication:
   replSetName: rs0

syncSource:
   replicaSet:
      hostAndPort: "mongod.search-community:27017"
      username: mongotUser
      passwordFile: /mongot-community/pwfile
      authSource: admin
      tls: false
      readPreference: primaryPreferred
storage:
   dataPath: "data/mongot"
server:
   grpc:
      address: "mongot.search-community:27028"
      tls:
         mode: "disabled"
metrics:
   enabled: true
   address: "mongot.search-community:9946"
healthCheck:
   address: "mongot.search-community:8080"
logging:
   verbosity: INFO

sections.heading: Step 5: Set up the Docker environment
sections.body:
To create the Docker network, run the following command:

```shell
docker network create search-community
```

sections.heading: Step 6: Start  and
sections.body:
Use the following steps to launch both services:

- Launch `mongod` and `mongot`:

  ```shell
  docker compose up -d
  ```

- Wait for both services to fully initialize.

  To monitor the logs of both services, run the following command:

  ```shell
  docker compose logs -f
  ```

  The initialization is complete when the logs return `MongoDB
  initialization completed`.

sections.heading: Step 7: Create a MongoDB Search index
sections.body:
- Connect to MongoDB with `mongosh`

  ```shell
  mongosh
  ```

- In the MongoDB shell, run the following commands to create a search
  index on the sample data

  ```javascript
  // Switch to the sample database
  use sample_mflix

  // Create a search index on the movies collection
  db.movies.createSearchIndex(
     "default",
     { mappings:
        { dynamic: true }
     }
  )
  ```

sections.heading: Step 8: Test your search functionality
